danger_signals.visual_form: Dragon character model in locations; destruction/scorch marks in city areas
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat to Ankh-Morpork; major danger state requiring player intervention

danger_signals.visual_form: NPCs in disguise or marked locations (Fool, Thief, Mason, etc.)
danger_signals.meaning: Hidden antagonists controlling dragon; localized threat requiring investigation

danger_signals.visual_form: Blocked pathways, sealed locations, guarded entrances
danger_signals.meaning: Danger zones or progression blockers requiring puzzle solutions

safety_signals.visual_form: University building; Archchancellor and wizard NPCs
safety_signals.meaning: Safe haven and quest-giver authority; institutional protection

safety_signals.visual_form: Item storage UI; Luggage as traveling companion
safety_signals.meaning: Player resource security and progression enablement
Information gathering points and safe social interaction

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: University building in Ankh-Morpork; magical academies and libraries (L-Space)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Golden control items (six golden artifacts tied to six members)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Brotherhood members in civilian disguises: Chucky the Fool, the Thief, the Mason, the Chimney Sweep, the Fishmonger, the Dunny King
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Brotherhood hideout (location unlocked via L-Space time travel); hidden throughout city social strata
Patrician's palace in Ankh-Morpork; administrative authority over city response to dragon

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Initially enslaved by Brotherhood; becomes ally after truth revealed; returns as consequence of Rincewind's actions
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Dragon lair (gold-filled location outside city); dragon sanctuary (Lady Ramkin's residence)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dragon visible in locations; destruction imagery; NPCs mention rampage
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Urgent tone from Archchancellor; citizens in fear; Patrician demands action

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Six golden control items visible/referenced; brotherhood members in assigned social roles
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Dragon remains enslaved and controllable; brotherhood members operate normally in city disguises

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Golden items removed from possession; dragon lair emptied of control artifacts
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Dragon becomes independent agent; initially seeks revenge on brotherhood; threatens Rincewind

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Transition to library memory space; time-displaced version of locations visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can witness theft of summoning book; gain access to brotherhood hideout through this knowledge

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Missing book from Unseen University library; replacement custard book in player inventory
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Brotherhood unable to summon dragon; prevents major destruction scenario

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Specific items in inventory: sword that goes 'ting', birthmark, magic spell, other heroic artifacts
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player gains combat readiness; positioned for final confrontation with dragon

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Lady Ramkin tied to rock as bait; dragon approaching sacrifice location in city square
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Final act pressure; Rincewind has acquired hero components but fails/hesitates in execution

environmental_storytelling.example: Unseen University communicates wizard authority and magical institutional power through its prominent placement and role as quest hub
Brotherhood members embedded in city trades (Thief, Mason, Chimney Sweep, Fishmonger, Fool, Dunny King) reveal political infiltration and systemic corruption narrative
Six golden items serve as both control mechanism and visual progression tracker; collecting them communicates player advancement toward freeing the dragon
Lady Ramkin's dragon sanctuary signals dragon domestication attempts and alternative viewpoints on dragon threat level
Library time-travel mechanic visually demonstrates knowledge preservation and historical investigation as story elements
Overworld map with progressively accessible regions shows world expansion and story pacing; regions become accessible upon story knowledge acquisition

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Reluctant hero; initially motivated by gold rather than civic duty
Authority figure demanding wizard involvement; hands dragon detector to Rincewind; later argues with Rincewind over dragon existence
Follows commands; causes destruction on schedule; appears aggressive and uncontrollable
Seeks revenge on Brotherhood first; then threatens Rincewind; will not return to native dimension
Maintain civilian disguises and assigned social roles; use golden items to control dragon; seek systemic change for personal benefit
Demands solutions; becomes annoyed at Rincewind's argument; assigns task to contain dragon threat
Captured and bound as dragon bait; represents civilian vulnerability and stakes elevation

notes: Discworld communicates world-state primarily through NPC behavior, dialogue context, and item-based progression mechanics rather than explicit visual danger indicators. The game uses faction embedding (Brotherhood disguised in civilian roles) to show political threat as systemic rather than obvious. The dual-control narrative (Brotherhood controlling dragon, then dragon seeking independence) creates shifting threat alignments. Rincewind's reluctant anti-hero positioning is reinforced behaviorally through self-interested motivations contrasting with institutional duty pressures. The use of L-Space as a temporal narrative device allows visual/gameplay access to past world-states, creating a non-linear world-state communication system.
